death.note: Per obituary 2016. Memorial at Episcopal Church of St. Matthew, San Mateo CA Oct 1, 2016.

narrative_blocks.header: Banker across five California institutions
narrative_blocks.body: He worked as a banker at United California Bank in Los Angeles, Middendorff Colgate & Co., Seattle First National Bank, Crocker National Bank, First Interstate Bank, and Wells Fargo. After banking he became chairman of Washington Square Associates and Cambridge Park Properties.

narrative_blocks.header: Marine Corps service from 1958 to 1961
narrative_blocks.body: He served in the United States Marine Corps from 1958 to 1961, between Cate School and his graduation from USC. He kept the 'Pops' nickname from his family throughout his banking career.

narrative_blocks.header: USC Man of Troy and Business School president
narrative_blocks.body: He was elected President of the USC Business School and received the university's Man of Troy Award. He had prepped at Cate School and Menlo School of Business before USC.

narrative_blocks.header: Pebble Beach summers with grandfather 'The Boss'
narrative_blocks.body: Rod's youth carried him between San Francisco, Hillsborough, and Indian Wells — but the family centerpiece was Pebble Beach, where his maternal grandfather Samuel F.B. Morse 'The Boss' had built the resort community itself. Summer visits with The Boss, brother Michael, and their mother Nancy Morse Hooker were the formative geography of two Hooker generations across the California coast.

cross_connections.display_label: Middle name 'Lent' continues his father's tribute to George.

education.notes: Attended in the 1950s under the Cate School name (the school had been The Santa Barbara School in earlier decades).
Later President of USC Business School alumni board; Man of Troy Award recipient.

institutions.institution_blurb: Member of San Francisco's oldest private gentlemen's club.
Member of the historic San Francisco Peninsula country club.

death.note: Per LegacyConnect record cited in obituary lookup; year 2016.

birth.historical_notes: Per Tony Hooker pedigree + Sam family knowledge: b.1909 — likely Hillsborough or San Francisco given father Robert Gay's residence pattern

marriages.notes: First wife (per WikiTree + 1940 census + family records). Two sons: Michael G. Hooker (Sam Talcott Hooker's father, b.~1936, NOT BUILT per living rule) and Rodman L. Hooker Jr. (HD3315, 1940-2016). Separated post-1940 census; Nancy went on to Walker then Borland. Rodman Lent later remarried.
Native of Pasadena; previously married Thomas Bunn of Pebble Beach (3 Bunn children). Died June 1, 1969 at home 761 Chiltern Rd Hillsborough age 54; coroner-investigated unattended death.
Third wife. Born Harrisburg PA 1925. Three Sollner children from prior marriage. No children with Rodman. Survived him by 14 years; d. Tampa FL Oct 5, 2008 age 83.

sources.short: Direct family information from Sam Talcott Hooker (curator)
sources.page: Sam Hooker confirmed: "Rodman Lent who was my grandfather"
'Grandson of Elizabeth Shreve and Robert Gay Hooker of Hillsborough'

narrative_blocks.header: First name echoes his Shreve grandmother's surname
narrative_blocks.body: His mother Elizabeth Lawton Shreve had carried Rodman as a maternal-line name through her own mother Rebecca Rodman Nichols. His uncle George Rodman Shreve, a partner in the Shreve & Co. SF jewelry firm, also carried it forward.

narrative_blocks.header: Three marriages: Morse, Austin, Sollner
narrative_blocks.body: He married Nancy Morse in July 1934 and had two sons Michael and Rodman Jr. before their post-1940 separation. He later married Jane Elise Austin (d. 1969 in Hillsborough) and Louise Henrichsen Sollner (d. 2008 in Tampa).

cross_connections.display_label: Middle name 'Lent' honors his father's business partner.

death.note: Last residence Pebble Beach. Buried Lake Forest Cemetery IL Section C, Block 15, Lot 13.

marriages.notes: Two sons: Michael G. Hooker (Sam Talcott Hooker's father, b.~1936, NOT BUILT per living-person rule) and Rodman L. Hooker Jr. (HD3315, 1940-2016). Per WikiTree: married July 1934. The 1940 census shows them as a couple in Hillsborough San Mateo County with sons Michael (4) and Rodman Jr. (0). Separated some time after the 1940 census; Nancy was with Walker by 1950.
One daughter Anne Walker (NOT BUILT). Walker had a daughter Martha from prior marriage to Alida Douglas Robinson. Walker d. Apr 8, 1966 Pebble Beach.
No children with Borland; he had four children from prior marriage. They lived at Pebble Beach from 1972 until her 2005 death.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter of Pebble Beach's founder Samuel Morse
narrative_blocks.body: She was born May 13, 1915 in San Francisco to Samuel Finley Brown Morse and Anne Camden Thompson, four years before her father founded Del Monte Properties at Pebble Beach. By the 1920 census her parents had separated and her mother had married Col. George Richardson.

narrative_blocks.header: Three husbands: Hooker, Walker, Borland
narrative_blocks.body: After her 1940-era separation from Rodman Lent Hooker she married New York socialite banker Kenneth Stewart Walker, who died at Pebble Beach in 1966. She then married William Fuller Borland on March 7, 1967 and lived at Pebble Beach until her 2005 death there.

narrative_blocks.header: Raised in Lake Forest by stepfather George Richardson
narrative_blocks.body: After her parents' 1916 separation she was raised by her mother and stepfather Col. George Adams Richardson in Lake Forest, Illinois from 1920. The family moved to Rancho Santa Fe in the early 1940s where Richardson ran the Rancho Santa Fe Inn.

narrative_blocks.header: Three marriages produced a blended California family
narrative_blocks.body: Nancy married first Rodman Lent Hooker — sons Michael and Rod Jr. — then Kenneth Stewart Walker, with daughter Anne Walker; and finally William Fuller Borland in 1967, who became stepfather to all three of her Hooker and Walker children. The blended family centered on Pebble Beach, where Nancy's father Samuel F.B. Morse 'The Boss' had built the resort community, and where Borland would chair the AT&T Pro-Am tournament in his retirement years.

death.note: Per Tony Hooker pedigree + Sam's data: lived Hillsborough San Mateo County ~10 years before death; lived San Mateo CA in 1939

burial.plot_notes: Cremated at Cypress Lawn Memorial Park Colma but NOT INTERRED there — disposition of cremains uncertain. Per Findagrave: 'Per Cypress Lawn Records, Robert Hooker was cremated at their facility but not interred there. You can call the office at 650-755-0580 to inquire of the disposition of cremains. Perhaps these children of Charles are in the family Mausoleum???'

sources.short: Tony Hooker pedigree (descendant chart for Thomas Hooker)

careers.role: Real estate agent / house broker / rent collector / insurance agent
careers.employer: Hooker & Lent (Robert G. Hooker + George H. Lent)
careers.location: 14 Post Street, San Francisco CA (telephone: Private Exchange 120)
careers.notes: Real estate agency, house brokerage, rent collection, and insurance.

Real estate and investment firm. Founded with brother-in-law George Heydenfeldt Lent (X02298, husband of his sister Bessie H04268) and brother-in-law Eugene Lent. The firm operated through at least 1932 per SF Real Estate Board Yearbook; building referenced in 1916 SF Commercial Club records. Robert G. Hooker was a Pacific-Union Club member (1932 roster) — same as his Lent business partners.
Per 1880s SF directory: 'Hooker Robert G., cashier Am. Sugar Refinery Co., r. 917 Bush' — early-career position before founding Hooker & Lent.

memberships.notes: One of the most exclusive private clubs in the western US. Robert was a member alongside his Lent business partners (his sister Bessie's husband George Heydenfeldt Lent and brother-in-law Eugene Lent).
One of the most exclusive private clubs in the western US. Per SFgenealogy 1932 roster.
Per Bohemian Club Officers & Members 1892 roster: 'HOOKER, Robert G.' — alongside brother G. Osgood Hooker and brother-in-law George H. Lent (married Bessie that same year).

Per Burlingame Country Club roster: 'HOOKER. ROBERT G.. Cunard Bldg.. 503 Market St.. San Francisco. Calif.' — listed alongside several other Hooker family members including 'Lent Hooker' (likely Rodman Lent Hooker HD3246), 'John R. Hooker' (likely John Rodman Hooker HD3243 at Curran & Dwyer), 'Osgood Hooker' (likely Osgood Hooker H05205 — C. Osgood's son), 'Mrs. C. Osgood Hooker' (Ella Wall Goodfellow Hooker, his sister-in-law widow), and 'Robert G. Hooker Jr.' (660 Market Street, Gladding McBean & Co).

narrative_blocks.header: Real estate broker with two brothers-in-law
narrative_blocks.body: He partnered with George and Eugene Lent in the Hooker & Lent firm at 503 Market Street in San Francisco. The firm operated through 1932 and built a long-running family-business network.

narrative_blocks.header: Studied mechanics at Berkeley, ended in real estate
narrative_blocks.body: He took a Ph.B. in Mechanics at the University of California, Berkeley. After early years as cashier at the American Sugar Refining Company, he settled into real estate brokerage.

narrative_blocks.header: Member of San Francisco's three elite clubs
narrative_blocks.body: He belonged to the Bohemian Club from 1892, the Pacific-Union Club, and the Burlingame Country Club. The Hooker family appeared on the Burlingame roster across three generations.

cross_connections.display_label: Brothers-in-law and partners in Hooker & Lent real estate.
Three brothers-in-law ran Hooker & Lent real estate firm.
Bore great-great-grandfather Bunker's Gay name into 1862 San Francisco
was his father-in-law; Founded Shreve & Co.

education.notes: Per Cal Berkeley alumni directory: 'Hooker, Robert Gay, Ph.B. (Mechanics), Real estate, 1117 Pine st, S F'

residences.source: 1880s SF directory (early career, cashier at Am. Sugar Refinery)

sources.short: Tony Hooker pedigree (descendant chart for Thomas Hooker)

cross_connections.display_label: Great-granddaughter Katherine carries her Shreve surname forward
Grandson Anthony carries her Shreve surname forward

death.note: Cremated; ashes scattered over his River Ranch home in Carmel.

parents.father_research_notes: George Washington Morse — Civil War soldier, later a lawyer in Massachusetts.

marriages.notes: Three children: Samuel F.B. Morse Jr. (1908-1956, not built), John Boit Morse (1910-1988, not built), Nancy Morse (I00288, b.1915 — Sam Talcott Hooker's grandmother). All three children raised in Lake Forest IL by Anne and her second husband George Adams Richardson.
One daughter Mary Morse Osborne Shaw (1920-2018, not built). Relda was previously married to a man named Stott.
No children. Maurine had been previously married to a man named Coburn.

notable.notable_blurb: Founder of Pebble Beach; 'Duke of Del Monte'

narrative_blocks.header: Founded Pebble Beach and the Del Monte Forest
narrative_blocks.body: He bought 7,000 acres on the Monterey Coast — including Pebble Beach, Pacific Grove, and the Hotel Del Monte — for $1.34 million in 1919, founding Del Monte Properties. He developed eight golf courses there including Spyglass Hill and Cypress Point while preserving the forest and coastline.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1907 football captain on undefeated team
narrative_blocks.body: He captained the undefeated 1906 Yale football team and was named to the 1906 All-America Team. His class voted him 'Most Popular' in 1907.

narrative_blocks.header: Ran the Crocker estate before liquidating Pacific Improvement
narrative_blocks.body: After Yale he managed the Crocker-Huffman ranch in Merced for W.H. Crocker. In 1916 he was made manager of the Pacific Improvement Company to liquidate its assets, which became the seed of Del Monte Properties.

narrative_blocks.header: Five-times cousin to the telegraph inventor Morse
narrative_blocks.body: Despite the matching name and middle initials, he was no closer than fifth cousin to the inventor of Morse code. The two shared great-great-great-great-grandparents.

narrative_blocks.header: Excluded Asians, Africans, Arabs and Jews by deed
narrative_blocks.body: Pebble Beach deed restrictions he agreed to in 1918 excluded 'Asians, Africans, Arabs and Jews' from owning property. His son-in-law Richard Osborne, then president of Del Monte Properties, removed the language in 1963.

education.notes: Attended Phillips Academy Andover prior to Yale College.
B.A. 1907. Captain of undefeated 1906 football team; member of 1906 All-America Team. Skull and Bones; Delta Kappa Epsilon. Voted Most Popular in graduating class.

institutions.institution_blurb: Member of the exclusive San Francisco private club.

death.note: Died at Palomar Hospital. Buried Fort Rosecrans National Cemetery, San Diego.

marriages.notes: Three Morse children: Samuel Jr (1908), John Boit (1910), Nancy (I00288, b.1915). All raised by Anne and second husband George Richardson.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Pebble Beach's founder at age nineteen
narrative_blocks.body: She married Samuel Finley Brown Morse on June 29, 1907 at Staatsburg-on-the-Hudson in Dutchess County, New York. They moved to California where he began his career, but the marriage ended in divorce by 1916.

narrative_blocks.header: Remarried within two years of leaving Morse
narrative_blocks.body: By the 1920 census she had married Col. George Adams Richardson and was living in Chicago with their three Morse children. Richardson would later be executor and trustee of the Marshall Field estate from 1922 until 1958.

narrative_blocks.header: Raised three Morse children in Lake Forest, Illinois
narrative_blocks.body: She and Richardson raised her three Morse children — Samuel Jr, John Boit, and Nancy — in Lake Forest from 1920 until they moved to Rancho Santa Fe in the early 1940s. The Richardsons owned and operated the Rancho Santa Fe Inn from 1941 to 1958.

narrative_blocks.header: Wheeling-born, Spence-schooled in New York City
narrative_blocks.body: She was born in Wheeling, Ohio County, West Virginia in 1888 and reared in New York City, where she attended the Spence School. She died at Palomar Hospital in San Diego County in December 1961, age 73.

education.school_name: The Spence School, New York City
education.notes: Reared in New York City; attended The Spence School.

INST097.primary_name: Cate School (formerly The Santa Barbara School / Miss Cate's School)
INST097.name: Cate School (formerly The Santa Barbara School / Miss Cate's School)
INST097.description: Cate School, founded 1910 in Santa Barbara CA as Miss Cate's School (also known as The Santa Barbara School for Boys / The Santa Barbara School), moved to Carpinteria CA in 1932 and was rebranded Cate School. An elite West Coast college-preparatory school. HD3243 John Rodman Hooker graduated 1921 (when called The Santa Barbara School) and later served as President of the Board of Trustees of the Alumni Association. HD3315 Rodman L. Hooker Jr. attended in the 1950s under the Cate School name.

sources.short: Golden Nugget Library — John R. Hooker biography

INST097.notes:
[type detail: category=educational_institution]
[type detail: sub_category=private_preparatory_school]
[research_notes] Two documented Hooker connections: HD3243 John Rodman Hooker (graduated 1921 when school was The Santa Barbara School; later Alumni Board President) and HD3315 Rodman L. Hooker Jr. (attended in the 1950s under the Cate School name). The institutional continuity from The Santa Barbara School to Cate School (1910→1932 reorganization in Carpinteria) was confirmed by the compiler in April 2026.

INST107.name: University of Southern California
INST107.notes: HD3315 Rodman L. Hooker Jr. attended USC; later served as President of the Business School alumni board and received the Man of Troy Award.

INST094.name: Pacific-Union Club

founding_year.founder: Merger of Pacific Club (founded 1852) and Union Club (founded 1854)

location.address: 1000 California Street (Flood Mansion atop Nob Hill since 1912)

INST094.description: The Pacific-Union Club is San Francisco's oldest and most exclusive private men's club, formed in 1889 by the merger of the Pacific Club (1852) and the Union Club (1854) — both gold-rush-era gentlemen's clubs. Since 1912 it has occupied the James C. Flood Mansion atop Nob Hill at 1000 California Street, the only Nob Hill mansion to survive the 1906 earthquake and fire. Membership has historically included San Francisco's mercantile, banking, legal, and political elite. The Hooker family carried multi-generational membership: the 1905 Officers and Members directory lists three Hooker descendants — Charles Gay Hooker (H02703, address 967 Bush), C. Osgood Hooker (H04265, his son), and R.G. Hooker (Robert Gay Hooker, H04266, address 14 Post). A generation later, Charles Gay's grandson John Rodman Hooker (HD3243, Hooker & Fay senior partner) was also a member per his Golden Nugget Library biography. The Hooker presence at the club thus spans four generations — and is presumed to have continued with John Rodman's son Anthony Shreve 'Tony' Hooker.
INST094.notes:
[merged from INST100]: Founded 1889 by merger of two earlier SF clubs (Pacific Club + Union Club). Houses in the historic James C. Flood Mansion atop Nob Hill — one of the few buildings to survive the 1906 earthquake. One of the most exclusive private clubs in the western US. Hooker family members documented: Eugene Lent (X02301, 1924+1932 rosters per SFgenealogy), Robert G. Hooker (H04266, 1932 roster). C. Osgood Hooker (H04265) likely also a member but specific roster confirmation pending.
[type detail: category=social_club]
[type detail: sub_category=private_club]
[research_notes] Three Hooker descendants documented as 1905 members: father Charles Gay (H02703), son C. Osgood (H04265), and son Robert Gay (H04266). The Hooker mercantile dynasty was an integral part of late-19th and early-20th-century SF business society. The Flood Mansion home of the club is itself a survivor of the 1906 earthquake — one of very few Nob Hill structures still standing — making the club a physical link to gold-rush-era San Francisco.

INST098.name: Burlingame Country Club

founding_year.founder: San Francisco's mercantile and railroad elite

INST098.description: Burlingame Country Club, founded 1893 in what is now Hillsborough San Mateo County, was the first country club established west of the Mississippi. Membership has historically drawn from San Francisco's banking, railroad, and mercantile elite. John Rodman Hooker (HD3243, Hooker & Fay brokerage senior partner) was a member per his Golden Nugget Library biography. His Hillsborough/San Mateo residence pattern — also recorded for his father Robert Gay Hooker's Hillsborough address per Sam Talcott Hooker's father's obituary — overlapped with Burlingame Country Club's geographic and social orbit.

sources.short: Golden Nugget Library — John R. Hooker biography

INST098.notes:
[merged from INST101]: Founded 1893; private country club in Hillsborough San Mateo County CA. The club's roster (year unknown) lists multiple members of the SF Hooker family clan — making it the densest single-institution Hooker-family roster outside immediate family functions: 'Mrs. C. Osgood Hooker' (Ella Wall Goodfellow Hooker, widow of H04265, Eucalyptus Road Burlingame); 'Osgood Hooker' (H05205, son of C. Osgood, 251 Kearny St SF); 'John R. Hooker' (likely HD3243 John Rodman Hooker, Kohl Bldg 486 California St, w/Curran & Dwyer); 'Robert G. Hooker' (H04266, Cunard Bldg 503 Market St); 'Robert G. Hooker Jr.' (Robert's son, 660 Market St, w/Gladding McBean & Co.); 'Lent Hooker' (likely Rodman Lent Hooker HD3246, 257 El Cerrito Ave San Mateo). Six Hooker-family members at the same club shows how thoroughly the family integrated into San Mateo Peninsula elite society.
[type detail: category=social_club]
[type detail: sub_category=country_club]
[research_notes] 1 documented Hooker member: HD3243 John Rodman Hooker. Future research may identify additional Hooker family members from the Hillsborough Robert Gay Hooker line (Sam Talcott Hooker's grandfather Rodman Lent Hooker likely lived in the same area).
